They first construct a version with constant-length nonlocal gates, then refine it to square-grid nearest-neighbor two-qubit gates without extra two-qubit depth beyond standard syndrome extraction. Simulations compare fault distances and logical error rates, finding comparable performance at d >= 5 near physical error rate 10^-3 despite a modest fault-distance reduction.
main_result: A nearest-neighbor twist-braiding logical S gate achieves 2d x d x d spacetime volume with logical error rates comparable to larger prior designs at relevant code distances.
application_angle: The protocol can lower space-time overhead for near-term fault-tolerant quantum processors and improve resource estimates for magic-state-based computation.

title: Quantum secret sharing in tripartite superconducting network
abstract: Superconducting microwave quantum networks is a rapidly developing field, enabling distributed quantum computing and holding a promise for hybrid architectures in quantum internet. Quantum secret sharing (QSS) is one of the key protocols for multipartite quantum networks and can provide an unconditionally secure way to share quantum states among $n$ players. Using microwave two-mode squeezed states as an entanglement resource, we experimentally implement a QSS protocol with $n = 3$, where a subset of at least $k = 2$ players must collaborate to faithfully reconstruct the original secret state. We demonstrate reconstructed-state fidelities that surpass the asymptotic no-cloning threshold of $F_\textrm{nc} = 2/3$ and identify a parameter regime that allows for unconditionally secure communication in the presence of an omnipotent dishonest player. Furthermore, we experimentally explore inherent connections between QSS and other important quantum information processing tasks, such as quantum dense coding and elementary quantum error correction of channel erasures. Finally, we discuss extensions of QSS and its relation to the concept of blind quantum computing.
one_liner: Experimentally demonstrates 2-of-3 quantum secret sharing in a superconducting microwave network.
why_it_matters: It is an application-level multipartite quantum communication protocol realized on superconducting hardware relevant to distributed quantum computing.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses microwave two-mode squeezed states as the entanglement resource for a tripartite superconducting quantum network. In the implemented n=3, k=2 threshold protocol, any authorized pair can collaborate to reconstruct the secret state with fidelities above the asymptotic no-cloning threshold. The authors also map connections to dense coding, elementary erasure-error correction, and security against a dishonest player in a favorable parameter regime.
main_result: A superconducting microwave platform realizes 2-of-3 quantum secret sharing with reconstructed fidelities exceeding the 2/3 no-cloning threshold.
application_angle: The result advances secure multipartite protocols for superconducting quantum links, distributed quantum processors, and early quantum-internet nodes.

title: Theory of spin qubits and the path to scalability
abstract: Spin qubits have emerged as a leading platform for quantum information processing due to their long coherence times, small footprint, and compatibility with the existing semiconductor industry. We first provide an introduction to the different qubit implementations currently being investigated, including single electron-spin qubits, hole-spin qubits, donor qubits, and multispin encodings. We discuss how the confinement and strain present in semiconductor heterostructures produce addressable levels whose spin degree of freedom can be used to encode a qubit. A large emphasis is placed on reviewing the theoretical foundations and recent experimental demonstrations of proposed mechanisms for long-range coupling, including hybrid approaches based on circuit QED and Andreev qubits, as well as spin shuttling. Finally, we review a recent proposal for linking spin qubits using topological spin textures.
one_liner: Review maps spin-qubit theory, implementations, and coupling routes needed for scalable semiconductor quantum processors.
why_it_matters: It consolidates the physics and engineering options for turning compact, CMOS-compatible spin qubits into connected large-scale hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper reviews electron, hole, donor, and multispin semiconductor spin-qubit encodings, emphasizing how confinement and strain define addressable spin states. It surveys long-range coupling mechanisms, including circuit-QED hybrids, Andreev-qubit approaches, spin shuttling, and proposed links mediated by topological spin textures. The focus is theoretical structure tied to recent experimental demonstrations and scalability constraints.
main_result: The work provides a unified theory-oriented roadmap of spin-qubit implementations and coupling mechanisms for scalable architectures.
application_angle: It helps hardware teams compare routes to dense, manufacturable, connected quantum processors using semiconductor fabrication infrastructure.

title: Spin Qubit Leapfrogging: Dynamics of shuttling electrons on top of another
abstract: Spin shuttling has crystalized as a powerful and promising tool for establishing intermediate-range connectivity in semiconductor spin-qubit devices. Although experimental demonstrations have performed exceptionally well on different materials platforms, the question of how to handle areas of low valley splitting in silicon during shuttling remains unresolved. In this work, we explore the possibility of utilizing the valley degree of freedom, particularly in regions of low valley splitting, to allow mobile spin qubits to be shuttled through an occupied stationary quantum dot, thereby leapfrogging over the stationary electron. This not only grants a more enriched mobility for shuttled electrons, as it opens new possible routing paths, but also enables the implementation of an entangling SWAP$^γ$ two-qubit gate operation in the process. Simulating this process for different sets of parameters, we demonstrate the feasibility of such an operation and offer a unique use case for otherwise precarious regions of a quantum processor chip and propose a possible extension to the set of possible operations for silicon spin qubit devices.
one_liner: Simulations show mobile silicon spin qubits can leapfrog occupied dots through valley states while enacting an entangling gate.
why_it_matters: The proposal turns problematic low-valley-splitting regions into routing and gate resources for more connected silicon spin-qubit chips.
detailed_summary: The paper studies electron shuttling in silicon quantum-dot arrays when the mobile spin encounters a stationary occupied dot in a low-valley-splitting region. By using the valley degree of freedom, the mobile qubit can pass through or over the occupied site, expanding routing possibilities. Simulations indicate that the process can also realize an entangling SWAP^gamma two-qubit operation under suitable parameters.
main_result: The authors demonstrate in simulation that valley-assisted leapfrogging can route a mobile spin qubit through an occupied dot and implement an entangling SWAP^gamma gate.
application_angle: This could improve intermediate-range connectivity and fault-tolerant layout flexibility in scalable silicon quantum processors.

title: Non-Hermitian reshaping of high-order Landau modes
abstract: When charged particles are subjected to strong magnetic fields, they form discrete energy levels known as Landau levels. The Landau levels consist of a series of degenerate states of Landau modes, making them a promising platform for large-capacity information processing. However, to date, exploiting the high-order Landau modes and control their spatial distributions has remained elusive. Here, we propose to construct magnetic fields, electric fields, and imaginary momentum simultaneously to reshape high-order Landau modes in non-Hermitian systems. By building a non-Hermitian electric circuit platform, we experimentally realize pseudomagnetic fields via inhomogeneous coupling and pseudoelectric fields via a gradient on-site potential, while simultaneously introducing an imaginary momentum via non-reciprocal coupling. We directly observe multi-frequency single-peak localization of high-order Landau modes. Our work provides a universal method for manipulating high-order Landau modes and exploring applications in nonHermitian systems, such as frequency multiplexing and wave packet reshaping.
one_liner: A non-Hermitian circuit platform reshapes high-order Landau modes into controllable multi-frequency localized peaks.
why_it_matters: Controlled high-order Landau modes could support denser photonic or wave-based information processing and multiplexing schemes.
detailed_summary: The authors propose simultaneous use of pseudomagnetic fields, pseudoelectric fields, and imaginary momentum to manipulate high-order Landau modes. In a non-Hermitian electric circuit, they implement inhomogeneous coupling, gradient on-site potential, and non-reciprocal coupling to emulate these ingredients. The experiment directly observes multi-frequency single-peak localization of high-order modes, showing spatial reshaping beyond conventional Landau-level control.
main_result: The work experimentally realizes controllable localization and reshaping of high-order Landau modes in a non-Hermitian circuit system.
application_angle: The method points toward wave-packet control and frequency-multiplexed signal processing in synthetic photonic and circuit platforms.

title: Phonon drag as a mechanism of delayed terahertz response of metals
abstract: We show that electron drag by nonequilibrium phonons describes the actual waveform and spectrum of terahertz pulses generated during femtosecond laser irradiation of metals. In contrast to previous models, there is a picosecond delay in the drag force development due to the relatively slow lattice heating and finite phonon lifetime. We also predict that, at high pump fluences, a macroscopic deformation wave enhances nonlinearly the drag force and terahertz response. Our results establish the terahertz pulse waveform as a direct probe of ultrafast lattice dynamics in metals.
one_liner: Phonon drag explains delayed terahertz emission from femtosecond-laser-excited metals and links waveforms to lattice heating.
why_it_matters: It reframes metal THz transients as probes of ultrafast lattice dynamics, useful for time-resolved materials sensing.
detailed_summary: The paper models terahertz pulse generation from laser-irradiated metals as electron drag driven by nonequilibrium phonons. The finite timescale of lattice heating and phonon lifetime produces a picosecond-delayed drag force, matching observed waveforms and spectra better than prior models. At high pump fluence, the authors predict that a macroscopic deformation wave nonlinearly enhances the THz response.
main_result: The authors show that delayed phonon drag can reproduce the waveform and spectrum of terahertz pulses emitted by laser-excited metals.
application_angle: THz waveform measurements could become a direct diagnostic for ultrafast lattice heating, phonon lifetimes, and deformation dynamics in metals.

title: Quantum Routing Beyond Pathfinding: Multipartite Entanglement Complementation
abstract: Conventional quantum routing operates under the entrenched assumption that pathfinding is a prerequisite for routing. This classical-inspired routing model imposes a restricting design option, which prevents scaling the quantumness to the network functioning. In this paper, we proposed a novel entanglement-driven routing framework that exploits multipartite entanglement complementation for enabling simultaneous 1-hop connectivity among all non-adjacent source-destination pairs. This changes the notion of ``remoteness'' in the entanglement graph, activated by entanglement. We extend this framework to inter-domain quantum networks and design a polynomial-time algorithm. Such an algorithm allows to select and parallelize multiple requests, bypassing NP-complete path discovery. Performance analysis shows the proposed routing strategy achieves up to $60\%$ hop reduction, with the algorithm enabling efficient parallelism and strong scalability in inter-domain quantum networks.
one_liner: Entanglement-complementation routing bypasses classical pathfinding to connect non-adjacent quantum-network pairs in parallel.
why_it_matters: It proposes a scalable quantum-native routing primitive that can reduce hops and avoid NP-complete path discovery bottlenecks.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces an entanglement-driven routing framework based on multipartite entanglement complementation rather than classical-style pathfinding. The approach can activate simultaneous one-hop connectivity among non-adjacent source-destination pairs in an entanglement graph. For inter-domain quantum networks, the authors give a polynomial-time request-selection and parallelization algorithm and report up to 60 percent hop reduction in performance analysis.
main_result: The work demonstrates a routing framework and polynomial-time inter-domain algorithm that parallelize requests while reducing effective hop count by up to 60 percent.
application_angle: It could improve throughput and scalability for future quantum internet control planes where entanglement itself is a routing resource.

title: Breakdown of spallation in multi-pulse ultrafast laser ablation
abstract: Ultrashort-pulse laser ablation of metals near damage threshold is governed by homogeneous spallation, in which tensile unloading releases a nanometre-thin liquid film whose optical signatures are temporally evolving concentric Newton rings in pump--probe experiments. This well-established picture rests almost exclusively on single-pulse results obtained on ideally flat surfaces, yet application-oriented processing invariably operates in a multi-pulse regime in which each pulse irradiates a surface progressively modified by preceding pulses. Whether homogeneous spallation persists under these conditions has remained an open question. Here we resolve this question using time-resolved pump-probe interferometry applied pulse by pulse to austenitic stainless steel. We show that homogeneous spallation dominates the first pulse, while its contribution is strongly reduced for the second pulse. By the third pulse, Newton rings vanish and sustained surface bulging collapses, with the optical transients fully saturating into a phase-explosion-like signature by the fourth pulse. Fourier-domain coherence analysis rules out roughness-induced decoherence as an optical artefact. Four independent observables, spanning time-resolved and final-state measurements, converge on the same transition after three to four pulses. Spallation-layer formation, widely invoked to explain ultrashort-pulse ablation of metals, is thus a single-pulse phenomenon rather than a multi-pulse ablation mechanism.
one_liner: Pump-probe interferometry shows metal spallation collapses after a few ultrafast laser pulses, giving way to phase-explosion behavior.
why_it_matters: It challenges a common ablation model in the multi-pulse regime most relevant to practical laser micromachining.
detailed_summary: The study uses pulse-by-pulse time-resolved pump-probe interferometry on austenitic stainless steel to track optical signatures of ultrashort-pulse ablation. The first pulse shows homogeneous spallation with Newton-ring signatures, but the second pulse strongly suppresses the effect and by the third to fourth pulse the response saturates into a phase-explosion-like transient. Fourier-domain coherence analysis and multiple observables indicate the transition is physical rather than an artifact of surface roughness.
main_result: The authors show that homogeneous spallation is mainly a first-pulse phenomenon and breaks down within three to four pulses in multi-pulse ablation.
application_angle: The result can improve predictive models and process control for industrial ultrafast laser machining of metals.

title: Decoupling of the STIRAP and Microwave-Dressing paths in Trapped Rydberg Ion Gates
abstract: The strong dipole-dipole interaction of trapped Rydberg ions offers the possibility of sub-microsecond entanglement gates. For example a two-qubit Control-Phase gate in 88 Sr + ions can be realized, by simultaneous excitation to the Rydberg states via stimulated Raman adiabatic passage (STIRAP) with simultaneous microwave induced dipole-dipole interaction. We show that this excitation protocol distorts the dark-state of the STIRAP stage and is prone to decay from the intermediate state. Here, we propose a novel pulse ordering, in which the STIRAP and the microwave dressing of the Rydberg states occurs in separate stages, preventing mutual interference effects that are detrimental to the gate fidelity. We show that, for experimentally feasible parameters, the proposed excitation scheme can achieve a fidelity of 99.93%, surpassing the experimentally demonstrated gate. In addition, we demonstrate a non-adiabatic speed-up to 400 ns by employing asymmetric pulse shapes in the STIRAP stage. The entangling phase is then controlled solely through the interaction strength by nonresonant asymmetric chirping of the microwave field.
one_liner: Separating STIRAP excitation from microwave dressing boosts simulated trapped-Rydberg-ion gate fidelity and enables 400 ns operation.
why_it_matters: The proposal targets faster, higher-fidelity entangling gates in trapped ions using pulse sequencing compatible with realistic Rydberg-ion parameters.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes a trapped Rydberg ion controlled-phase gate where simultaneous STIRAP excitation and microwave-induced dipole-dipole dressing distort the STIRAP dark state. It proposes a staged pulse ordering that decouples optical excitation from microwave dressing, reducing intermediate-state decay channels. With feasible parameters, simulations reach 99.93% fidelity and show a non-adiabatic 400 ns variant using asymmetric STIRAP pulses and chirped microwave control.
main_result: A decoupled STIRAP/microwave protocol is predicted to realize a 99.93% fidelity trapped-ion Rydberg phase gate, with a 400 ns accelerated option.
application_angle: Improved fast entangling gates would support higher-throughput trapped-ion quantum processors with reduced gate-error budgets.

title: Experimental realisation of topological spin textures in a Penning trap
abstract: Quantum simulation with controllable many-body platforms offers a powerful route to exploring complex phases and dynamics that are difficult to access in natural materials. Among these, topological spin textures such as skyrmions are central to modern condensed-matter physics and play a key role in chiral quantum many-body systems. Their controlled realisation in large, programmable quantum platforms, however, remains an outstanding challenge. Here, we report deterministic generation and site-resolved reconstruction of topological spin textures in a two-dimensional crystal of more than 150 trapped ions. Using globally applied spin-dependent forces, we generate skyrmion configurations and reconstruct the full vector spin field with single-ion resolution, obtaining a winding number of 0.99$\pm$0.02 and a mean local fidelity of 0.87$\pm$0.04. In addition, we implement single-ion-resolved control to deterministically prepare domain-wall states, extending our approach to a broader class of non-uniform spin textures. These results establish trapped-ion crystals as a platform for engineering complex spin textures and open the door to exploring topology-dependent nonequilibrium dynamics in long-range interacting quantum systems.
one_liner: A Penning-trap ion crystal deterministically prepares and images skyrmion-like spin textures with single-ion resolution.
why_it_matters: It demonstrates programmable topological spin textures in a large, controllable quantum simulator rather than inferring them indirectly from bulk observables.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses a two-dimensional crystal of more than 150 trapped ions in a Penning trap and globally applied spin-dependent forces to generate topological spin textures. The authors reconstruct the full vector spin field with single-ion resolution, measuring a winding number of 0.99 +/- 0.02 and mean local fidelity of 0.87 +/- 0.04. They also use single-ion-resolved control to prepare domain-wall states, extending the method beyond skyrmion configurations.
main_result: The work experimentally realizes and reconstructs programmable skyrmion and domain-wall spin textures in a large trapped-ion array.
application_angle: This creates a hardware route to studying topology-dependent nonequilibrium dynamics and chiral many-body physics in engineered quantum matter.

title: High-gain and large-bandwidth Josephson parametric amplifier influenced by Fabry-Pérot interference
abstract: Quantum-limited parametric amplifiers are essential components for many quantum technologies operating in the microwave domain. Achieving both high gain and broad bandwidth, however, remains challenging due to trade-offs between gain and bandwidth, pump efficiency, and dynamic range. Moreover, high-gain broadband amplifiers become increasingly sensitive to their external electromagnetic environment, which can distort their gain spectra and hinder reliable operation. Here, we present an accurate theoretical model and a systematic design methodology for a flux-driven, lumped-element Josephson parametric amplifier based on a SQUID array. Our device achieves near-quantum-limited, phase-preserving amplification with a net gain of 20 (maximally 44) dB and a 3-dB bandwidth of $\sim$50 ($\lesssim$0.2) MHz. We further show that the gain spectra exhibit pronounced sensitivity to weak reflections in the input-output waveguide caused by impedance mismatches in the microwave environment. By incorporating Fabry-Pérot-type interference into a quantum input-output model, we analytically reproduce these complex spectral features and identify how they depend on the physical parameters of the environment. More generally, our results provide a practical framework for separating the intrinsic dynamics of parametric amplifiers from environmental effects. This approach enables reliable characterization and optimization of amplifier performance while providing a systematic strategy for diagnosing microwave reflections and engineering environmental interference to shape amplifier gain spectra, thereby offering a pathway toward robust, reproducible, and truly quantum-limited microwave amplification.
one_liner: A SQUID-array Josephson parametric amplifier reaches high gain and useful bandwidth while modeling Fabry-Perot spectral distortions.
why_it_matters: It links amplifier performance limits to realistic microwave-environment reflections, giving a practical path to reproducible quantum-limited readout hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper presents a flux-driven lumped-element Josephson parametric amplifier based on a SQUID array, together with a design and modeling framework. The device achieves near-quantum-limited phase-preserving amplification with 20 dB net gain, up to 44 dB maximum gain, and about 50 MHz 3 dB bandwidth at 20 dB gain. The authors show that weak impedance mismatches in the microwave environment create Fabry-Perot-type interference that strongly shapes gain spectra, and they reproduce these features using a quantum input-output model.
main_result: The amplifier demonstrates near-quantum-limited microwave amplification while a Fabry-Perot-aware model explains complex gain-spectrum distortions.
application_angle: Better diagnosis and engineering of microwave reflections can improve qubit readout chains and other cryogenic quantum microwave systems.

title: Time-resolved SNOM via phase-domain sampling
abstract: Time-resolved scanning near-field optical microscopy (tr-SNOM) enables the measurement of the dynamic optical response of functional surfaces beyond the diffraction limit. Experimental challenges are imposed both by the use of a pulsed light source, and by the need for interferometric signal modulation to isolate the near-field contribution. We present a novel experimental approach to retrieve the tr-SNOM signal using a 200 kHz laser system and pseudo-heterodyne modulation. We circumvent the Nyquist limit for spectral demodulation by sampling modulation phases, pump intensity and SNOM signal for every laser shot. A general time-resolved SNOM signal is derived, independent of detection scheme or physical assumptions about the near-field enhancement, and is successfully measured and isolated on WS$_2$ monolayer and multilayer regions. We confirm localization by signal-distance curves, spatial confinement at material boundaries, and by identifying signal contributions at individual modulation harmonics. Disentangling the dynamic contributions enables us to extract the dynamic dielectric function of the sample. Showing the capability of phase-domain sampling paves the way to integration of more diverse and specialized light sources, growing the potential of optical ultrafast near-field measurements.
one_liner: Phase-domain sampling enables time-resolved SNOM with a 200 kHz laser while isolating ultrafast near-field signals in WS2.
why_it_matters: The method relaxes timing and demodulation constraints in tr-SNOM, expanding compatible ultrafast light sources for nanoscale optical dynamics.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a time-resolved SNOM method that records modulation phase, pump intensity, and SNOM signal for every laser shot with a 200 kHz source. By sampling in the phase domain, the approach bypasses the usual Nyquist constraint for spectral demodulation in pseudo-heterodyne detection. Measurements on monolayer and multilayer WS2 isolate localized dynamic near-field responses, validate confinement through distance curves and boundaries, and extract the dynamic dielectric function.
main_result: The work demonstrates phase-domain tr-SNOM signal recovery and dynamic near-field dielectric extraction on WS2 samples.
application_angle: It supports ultrafast nanoscale optical sensing of functional materials using more diverse laser systems and detection schemes.

title: Dynamic rephasing in a telecom warm vapor quantum memory
abstract: The Off-Resonant Cascaded Absorption (ORCA) protocol in warm atomic vapors offers a scalable platform for high-bandwidth, low noise quantum memories, but its coherence time is fundamentally limited by Doppler-induced dephasing. We introduce and experimentally demonstrate a dynamic rephasing protocol that counteracts Doppler dephasing in a telecom-band ORCA quantum memory. By transferring the stored excitation to an auxiliary shelving state, we effectively reverse the accumulated Doppler phase and extend the storage time by a factor of 50 while preserving the memory's GHz bandwidth and low noise. Using this protocol, we then demonstrate on-demand storage and retrieval of four independent time-bin modes within a single warm vapor memory -- showing that Doppler dephasing can alternatively be harnessed for high-dimensional temporal mode processing. Our results establish rephasing in warm atomic vapors as a viable route toward high-bandwidth, temporally multiplexed quantum memories operating at room temperature.
one_liner: Dynamic rephasing extends a telecom warm-vapor ORCA quantum memory by 50x while retaining GHz bandwidth and low noise.
why_it_matters: Room-temperature, telecom-band, multiplexed quantum memories are key components for deployable quantum networking and photonic processing.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates a dynamic rephasing protocol for an Off-Resonant Cascaded Absorption quantum memory in warm atomic vapor. By transferring the stored excitation to an auxiliary shelving state, the protocol reverses Doppler-induced phase accumulation and extends coherence time by a factor of 50. The same system preserves GHz bandwidth and low noise, and supports on-demand storage and retrieval of four independent time-bin modes in one memory.
main_result: Dynamic rephasing experimentally achieves 50-fold longer storage and four-mode time-bin operation in a telecom warm-vapor memory.
application_angle: The result advances practical high-bandwidth quantum memories for temporal multiplexing in room-temperature quantum communication links.

title: Optimally Controlled Storage of a Qubit in an Inhomogeneous Spin Ensemble
abstract: The storage of quantum information in spin-ensembles is limited by practically unavoidable inhomogeneous broadening, and the macroscopic number of spins in such an ensemble makes the design of control solutions to increase the coherence time a challenging task. Together with a concurrently developed Krylov theory that allows us to treat the control problem efficiently, we design optimal cavity modulation for such spin ensembles that achieve an order of magnitude enhancement in qubit lifetime compared to the losses due to inhomogeneity and cavity decay.
one_liner: Optimal cavity modulation extends qubit storage in an inhomogeneously broadened spin ensemble by about an order of magnitude.
why_it_matters: It addresses a central hardware bottleneck for ensemble memories: preserving qubits despite broad spin distributions and cavity loss.
detailed_summary: The paper studies qubit storage in spin ensembles where inhomogeneous broadening causes dephasing and limits memory lifetime. Using an efficient Krylov-theory-based control treatment, the authors design optimal cavity modulation without directly simulating every microscopic spin degree of freedom. The optimized controls provide an order-of-magnitude lifetime enhancement compared with limits from inhomogeneity and cavity decay.
platform_or_system: inhomogeneous spin ensemble in a cavity
main_result: Optimal cavity modulation is shown to substantially prolong stored-qubit lifetime in a broadened spin-ensemble memory model.
application_angle: The method is relevant to ensemble-based quantum memories and hybrid quantum hardware where scalable control must counter disorder-induced dephasing.

title: Quantum information spreading in inhomogeneous spin ensembles
abstract: We present a Krylov space based theoretical framework for modeling inhomogeneous spin ensembles with arbitrary distributions of spin frequencies and couplings. The framework is then used to asymptotically large spin ensemble. In the single-excitation subspace, the Krylov construction allows for to derive exact expressions for the Lieb-Robinson velocity and quantum speed limit, and figure of merit such as Krylov complexity. Our work reveals a strong dependence of the speed of information flow on the statistical distribution of resonance frequencies in the spin ensemble with immediate implications for the design of components for quantum technologies, realized for example with nitrogen vacancy centers, nuclear spins or ultracold atoms.
one_liner: Krylov-space theory predicts information-spreading speeds in inhomogeneous spin ensembles with arbitrary frequency and coupling distributions.
why_it_matters: The framework links disorder statistics to Lieb-Robinson velocities and quantum speed limits, informing spin-ensemble quantum memory and sensor design.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a Krylov-space framework for single-excitation dynamics in large inhomogeneous spin ensembles with arbitrary resonance-frequency and coupling distributions. It derives exact asymptotic expressions for information-spreading measures including Lieb-Robinson velocity, quantum speed limits, and Krylov complexity. The analysis shows that the statistical distribution of spin frequencies strongly controls how fast quantum information propagates through the ensemble.
main_result: A theoretical framework gives exact information-spreading bounds and metrics for asymptotically large inhomogeneous spin ensembles.
application_angle: The results guide engineering of ensemble-based quantum components such as NV-center, nuclear-spin, and ultracold-atom platforms.

title: High-power-handling ultra-compact acousto-optic modulators using one-dimensional topological interface states on thin-film lithium tantalate
abstract: Recent advances in integrated photonics have enabled on-chip signal modulation and processing through localized photon-phonon interactions. For acousto-optic devices, compact footprint and high efficiency are essential for dense integration, while strong power handling is critical for stable operation in demanding applications. However, it remains challenging to achieve these features simultaneously on existing integrated platforms. Here, we propose and experimentally demonstrate, for the first time on a thin-film lithium tantalate platform, an ultra-compact acousto-optic modulator based on topological interface states. Benefiting from the strong optical confinement of the topological boundary state, the device achieves a footprint of 0.13 by 0.12 mm2 and a half-wave voltage-length product of 0.491 Vcm. We further demonstrate stable acousto-optic modulation at an on-chip optical power of up to 28 dBm (630.9 mW), highlighting the strong power-handling capability of the thin-film lithium tantalate topological structure. This work provides a compact and high-power solution for microwave-to-photonic transduction and shows the potential of the thin-film lithium tantalate for robust integrated photonic systems.
one_liner: Thin-film lithium tantalate topological interface states enable a compact, efficient, high-power acousto-optic modulator.
why_it_matters: It combines small footprint, low drive metric, and 630.9 mW on-chip optical power handling for robust integrated microwave-photonic systems.
detailed_summary: The work experimentally demonstrates an ultra-compact acousto-optic modulator on thin-film lithium tantalate using one-dimensional topological interface states. Strong optical confinement from the topological boundary mode yields a 0.13 by 0.12 mm2 footprint and a half-wave voltage-length product of 0.491 Vcm. The device remains stable under up to 28 dBm, or 630.9 mW, of on-chip optical power.
main_result: The demonstrated modulator achieves compact 0.0156 mm2 area, 0.491 Vcm efficiency, and stable operation at 28 dBm on-chip optical power.
application_angle: This is relevant for dense, high-power integrated microwave-to-photonic transduction and signal processing.

title: Three-dimensional photon transport in spinodal photocatalytic aerogels: how bicontinuous morphology controls kinetic rate constants
abstract: Porous monolithic photocatalysts based on anatase TiO2 in silica aerogels are promising for air purification. Their bicontinuous spinodal architecture offers high surface area and strong light scattering. However, extracting intrinsic kinetic rates requires accurate optical models. Current methods replace the complex 3D pore network with a homogeneous 1D slab, an approximation whose error is unknown for spinodal geometries. We combine 3D spinodal masks from Cahn-Hilliard simulations with GPU Monte Carlo photon transport to quantify this. We introduce a solid-phase fluence estimator that accounts for catalytic site distribution, comparing it to volume averages and diffusion approximations. The solid phase receives 50% more photons than volume averages at porosity 0.70, rising to 70% at 0.90. This preferential illumination stems from quasi-ballistic paths through pore channels, termed photon channelling. The extracted kinetic descriptor differs by 34% between 3D Monte Carlo and diffusion models. Homogeneous controls show that roughly 50% of the total 73% discrepancy is intrinsic to the bicontinuous structure and cannot be fixed by effective medium theories. These results provide the first quantitative correction for kinetic extraction in such photocatalysts and establish design rules linking synthesis coarsening, pore size, and light efficiency.
one_liner: 3D Monte Carlo photon transport quantifies how spinodal aerogel morphology biases illumination and photocatalytic rate extraction.
why_it_matters: It gives practical correction factors and design rules for porous photocatalysts where 1D or effective-medium optical models misestimate kinetics.
detailed_summary: The paper combines Cahn-Hilliard-generated 3D spinodal masks with GPU Monte Carlo photon transport to model light propagation in TiO2-silica aerogel photocatalysts. A solid-phase fluence estimator shows that catalytic solids receive 50% more photons than volume averages at 0.70 porosity and 70% more at 0.90 porosity. The authors identify quasi-ballistic photon channelling through pore channels and find a 34% kinetic-descriptor difference between 3D Monte Carlo and diffusion models.
main_result: 3D morphology causes preferential solid-phase illumination and large kinetic-extraction errors relative to diffusion and homogeneous approximations.
application_angle: The model supports better design and calibration of porous photocatalysts for air purification and light-driven chemical processing.

title: Dimensioning of Quantum Memories for Distilled Quantum EPR Packets
abstract: The quantum Internet envisions a network where information is transmitted through entanglement, with Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen (EPR) pairs serving as one of the fundamental carriers. In this work, we propose a framework for dimensioning quantum memories capable of storing distilled EPR pairs useful to transmitting and manage quantum error correcting codes. Using a Markov chain model, we capture the stochastic evolution of stored entangled states in quantum memories, linking memory performance to system parameters such as technology characteristics and initial entanglement fidelity. Building on this framework, we provide analytical tools and design principles for optimizing memory architectures that preserve high-fidelity entanglement over time, ensuring the availability of encoded quantum resources necessary for several operations in future quantum Internet infrastructures transmitting EPR packets.
one_liner: A Markov-chain framework dimensions quantum memories that store distilled EPR pairs for quantum Internet packet operations.
why_it_matters: It turns stochastic memory degradation and entanglement fidelity into design rules for maintaining encoded network resources.
detailed_summary: The work proposes analytical tools for sizing quantum memories that hold distilled EPR pairs used in EPR-packet transmission and management of quantum error-correcting codes. A Markov-chain model captures the stochastic evolution of stored entangled states and relates memory performance to technology parameters and initial fidelity. The framework yields design principles for preserving high-fidelity entanglement over time in future quantum network infrastructure.
main_result: The paper provides a Markov-chain-based method to dimension memories for stored distilled EPR resources under realistic degradation.
application_angle: It helps specify memory capacity and quality requirements for quantum Internet nodes that must buffer usable entanglement.

title: Redefining the limits of real-time noise cancellation in optical fiber links
abstract: A broad and growing array of applications rely on the faithful transmission of ultrastable optical signals over noisy paths, requiring cancellation of environmentally induced noise. A generally accepted limit constrains how well the path length noise can be suppressed in real time. Here, we show that this standard limit is not fundamental and can be improved upon. By considering the temporal correlations between the round-trip and one-way signals, we develop a new framework for optimizing the noise cancellation feedback signal for any spatial distribution of noise along the signal path. We use this framework to surpass the standard limit in two sets of experiments. First, we demonstrate noise cancellation in a deployed urban optical fiber, where we achieve noise suppression approximately 6 dB beyond the standard limit. Then, in a reconfigurable lab-based fiber-optic testbed, we show that, for certain spatial distributions of noise, suppression of well over 10 dB beyond the standard limit is readily achievable. With the use of digital signal processing to generate the correction signal, our new technique requires no new electro-optic hardware relative to the field-standard noise cancellation scheme. This will allow for widespread adoption of these improved limits in existing systems, with applications in optical clock distribution, optical clock comparisons for fundamental physics and geodesy, and quantum networking.
one_liner: Correlation-aware feedback beats the standard real-time noise-cancellation limit in optical fiber frequency links.
why_it_matters: The method improves deployed fiber performance by about 6 dB, can exceed 10 dB in tailored links, and uses only digital signal processing changes.
detailed_summary: The paper shows that the commonly accepted real-time path-length noise cancellation limit in optical fiber links is not fundamental. By exploiting temporal correlations between round-trip and one-way signals, it optimizes the feedback correction for arbitrary spatial noise distributions. Experiments achieve about 6 dB beyond the standard limit in deployed urban fiber and more than 10 dB beyond it in a reconfigurable lab testbed for favorable noise profiles.
main_result: A DSP-based cancellation framework surpasses the standard real-time suppression limit without adding new electro-optic hardware.
application_angle: The technique can upgrade optical clock distribution, clock comparisons, geodesy links, and quantum-network timing or frequency transfer.

title: From coupled $\mathbb{Z}_3$ Rabi models to the $\mathbb{Z}_3$ Potts model
abstract: We study $\mathbb{Z}_3$-symmetric Rabi model that describes a three-level system coupled to two bosonic modes. We derive a mapping of the two-mode $\mathbb{Z}_3$ Rabi model onto a qubit-boson ring. This mapping allows us to formulate a realistic implementation of the $\mathbb{Z}_3$ Rabi model based on superconducting qubits. It also provides context for the previously proposed optomechanical implementation of the $\mathbb{Z}_3$ Rabi model. In addition, we propose a physical implementation of the $\mathbb{Z}_3$ Potts model via a coupled chain of $\mathbb{Z}_3$ Rabi models.
one_liner: A superconducting-qubit route maps coupled Z3 Rabi models onto hardware and extends them to a Z3 Potts chain.
why_it_matters: It translates exotic Z3-symmetric light-matter physics into realistic circuit-QED building blocks for quantum simulation.
detailed_summary: The paper studies a Z3-symmetric Rabi model consisting of a three-level system coupled to two bosonic modes. It derives a mapping from the two-mode Z3 Rabi model to a qubit-boson ring, enabling a realistic superconducting-qubit implementation and contextualizing earlier optomechanical proposals. The authors further propose realizing the Z3 Potts model using a coupled chain of Z3 Rabi models.
main_result: The work maps Z3 Rabi physics to implementable qubit-boson hardware and proposes a coupled-chain route to the Z3 Potts model.
application_angle: It offers a circuit-QED architecture for analog quantum simulation of discrete-symmetry many-body models.

title: Distributed quantum-classical hybrid algorithm for solving K-SAT problem
abstract: Recently, Dunjko et al.(PRL, 2018) proposed an algorithm for accelerating the solution of 3-satisfiability problems using a small-scale quantum computer. In this paper, we design a distributed quantum-classical hybrid algorithm for solving K-satisfiability problems. Under resource-constrained conditions, our algorithm achieves a significant acceleration in the core term of the exponential time complexity. The proposed algorithm is a generalization of the algorithm by Dunjko et al. Compared with their algorithm, our algorithm requires a smaller number of qubits. More importantly, the proposed algorithm does not rely on any quantum communication.
one_liner: A distributed hybrid quantum-classical K-SAT solver reduces qubit needs and accelerates the exponential core without quantum links.
why_it_matters: It targets near-term distributed use of small quantum processors for hard combinatorial search while avoiding quantum communication overhead.
detailed_summary: The paper generalizes the Dunjko et al. quantum-assisted SAT approach from 3-SAT to K-SAT and distributes the workload across resource-limited quantum-classical nodes. The design reduces the number of qubits required per processor while preserving acceleration in the dominant exponential term. A notable systems point is that the distributed architecture does not require quantum communication between nodes.
main_result: The authors propose a distributed hybrid K-SAT algorithm with lower qubit requirements and accelerated exponential-core runtime terms without quantum communication.
application_angle: The scheme is relevant to deploying quantum-assisted optimization on constrained near-term hardware connected only by classical networks.

title: Simulating the dynamics of an SU(2) matrix model on a trapped-ion quantum computer
abstract: Matrix models are an important class of systems in string theory and theoretical physics, with applications to random matrix theory, quantum chaos, and black holes. Hamiltonian Monte Carlo simulations and gauge/gravity duality have been used to study these systems at thermal equilibrium, and the bootstrap program has been used to efficiently determine operator expectation values by imposing positivity constraints. However, simulating real-time, non-equilibrium dynamics remains a fundamental challenge. In this work, we present the first digital quantum simulation of a bosonic matrix model, executed on the Quantinuum System Model H2 trapped-ion quantum computer. We focus on an $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ gauge theory with a quartic potential as it is simple enough to validate against exact classical solutions and yet complex enough to reflect the non-local structure of larger theories. Using the Loschmidt echo as our primary dynamical observable, we systematically decompose simulation errors into three distinct sources: Hilbert space truncation, Trotterization, and hardware noise. We demonstrate a new post-selection scheme that detects and discards gauge-symmetry violations in the Fock basis and show that at small scales it, along with zero-noise extrapolation, can give modest improvements in fidelity. These approaches struggle to scale to larger system sizes in their current implementations, emphasizing the need to move beyond them and to focus on depth reduction through improved compilation and unitary synthesis, and run-time error handling such as additional error suppression, error detection, as well as error correction approaches. This work establishes a foundation for extending digital quantum simulation to more complex matrix models -- revealing that fundamental challenges in qubit resources and circuit depth remain formidable obstacles for scaling to holographically interesting regimes.
one_liner: A trapped-ion processor digitally simulates real-time dynamics of an SU(2) bosonic matrix model and benchmarks key error sources.
why_it_matters: It is an application-relevant hardware demonstration for quantum simulation of gauge-structured models beyond equilibrium methods.
detailed_summary: The work implements the first digital quantum simulation of a bosonic matrix model on Quantinuum System Model H2. It studies an SU(2) gauge theory with a quartic potential and uses Loschmidt echoes to compare hardware dynamics against exact classical results. The authors separate errors from Hilbert-space truncation, Trotterization, and hardware noise, and test gauge-violation post-selection plus zero-noise extrapolation.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates real-time digital simulation of a small SU(2) matrix model and quantifies how truncation, Trotter, and hardware errors limit fidelity.
application_angle: The results map concrete compilation, depth, and error-handling bottlenecks for scaling quantum simulation toward more complex gauge and holographic models.

title: Protecting Heisenberg scaling in quantum metrology via engineered dressed states
abstract: Quantum metrology promises precision beyond classical limits but environmental noise, unless properly controlled, reduces the quantum advantage to at most a constant improvement. A key challenge is therefore to design quantum control strategies that suppress noise while preserving sensitivity to the targeted signal. Here, we suggest to use dressed states generated by static fields to achieve this goal and show that success of this strategy depends on the spectral properties of the environment. For low-temperature noise, we show that Heisenberg scaling can be achieved if and only if the signal generator lies outside the linear span of the system-environment coupling operators. This implies that the proper dressed states may enable Heisenberg scaling even in cases where the well-known Hamiltonian-not-in-Lindblad-span criterion, evaluated without dressing, would forbid it. We illustrate dressed state metrology for the example of NV-center thermometry under magnetic-field fluctuations, with the framework readily applicable to other platforms.
one_liner: Engineered dressed states can preserve Heisenberg scaling in metrology when the signal escapes the dressed noise span.
why_it_matters: It gives a practical control route for maintaining quantum-enhanced sensing under structured low-temperature noise.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes how static-field-generated dressed states can suppress environmental noise while retaining sensitivity to a target signal. For low-temperature environments, it proves Heisenberg scaling is possible exactly when the signal generator lies outside the span of the system-environment coupling operators after dressing. The authors illustrate the framework using NV-center thermometry affected by magnetic-field fluctuations.
main_result: The authors show engineered dressed states can restore Heisenberg scaling in cases where an undressed Lindblad-span test would rule it out.
application_angle: This suggests experimentally accessible field engineering strategies for robust nanoscale thermometry and related quantum sensors.

title: Non-symmetric quantum interfaces with bilayer atomic arrays
abstract: We study quantum light-matter interfaces based on bilayer atomic arrays in free space, considering interlayer spacings $a_z$ that may deviate from the Bragg-symmetric condition, $a_z\in \mathrm{integer}\times λ/2$ with $λ$ the light wavelength. Mapping the problem to a one-dimensional model, we show that the interface efficiency is fully determined by simple scattering observables $-$ reflection and transmission $-$ providing a direct, experimentally accessible characterization. This reveals new opportunities for optimizing light-matter coupling by operating beyond the Bragg symmetry. In particular, we identify configurations that suppress diffraction losses via destructive interference, enabling substantially improved interface efficiencies compared to Bragg-constrained designs. In addition, we introduce a new quantum memory scheme based on a collective dark state whose coupling to light is continuously controlled by tuning the interlayer spacing. More broadly, our results establish non-symmetric atomic arrays as a flexible platform for efficient quantum interfaces in free space.
one_liner: Bilayer atomic arrays operated away from Bragg symmetry can suppress diffraction loss and improve free-space quantum interfaces.
why_it_matters: It provides experimentally measurable design rules for more efficient atom-light interfaces and controllable quantum memories.
detailed_summary: The study examines free-space quantum interfaces formed by two atomic layers with interlayer spacings not restricted to integer half-wavelength Bragg conditions. By mapping the interface to a one-dimensional scattering model, it relates efficiency directly to reflection and transmission observables. The authors identify non-symmetric geometries where destructive interference suppresses diffraction losses and propose a memory based on a collective dark state controlled by layer spacing.
main_result: The paper shows non-Bragg bilayer arrays can achieve higher interface efficiency and enable a spacing-tunable collective-dark-state memory.
application_angle: The approach could improve quantum network nodes by making atom-photon coupling and memory operation more efficient in free space.

title: Scalable quantum error correction tailored for a heavy-hex qubit array
abstract: To produce an operable quantum computer that is made with imperfect hardware, we must design and test scalable quantum error correcting codes that are suited for the devices we can build and, in unison, develop decoding strategies that accommodate device-specific noise characteristics. Here, we introduce the \emph{dynamic compass code}, a subsystem code with a novel syndrome extraction cycle, that has a competitive threshold while making efficient use of qubits arranged on a heavy-hex lattice. We use a superconducting qubit array to implement a distance-5 instance of this code, and demonstrate how detailed noise characterisation can boost decoder performance to yield significant improvements in logical error rates. We perform averaged circuit eigenvalue sampling (ACES) to acquire detailed context-dependent error information on all elements of the syndrome extraction process. Furthermore, we leverage soft information produced from measurement devices to augment the decoder with measurement error information and detect leakage errors for exclusion through post-selection. Our noise-informed approach yields up to 38.3\% improvement in the logical error rate of a distance-5 implementation of the dynamic compass code in experiment.
one_liner: A distance-5 dynamic compass code on a heavy-hex superconducting array improves logical errors using noise-informed decoding.
why_it_matters: It is a concrete hardware-tailored QEC advance for the heavy-hex layouts used in scalable superconducting processors.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce the dynamic compass code, a subsystem code with a syndrome extraction cycle designed for heavy-hex qubit connectivity. They implement a distance-5 instance on a superconducting qubit array and characterize context-dependent syndrome-circuit errors using ACES. The decoder is enhanced with noise characterization, soft measurement information, and leakage detection with post-selection, yielding experimentally improved logical error rates.
main_result: A noise-informed distance-5 dynamic compass code implementation achieves up to 38.3% logical error-rate improvement in experiment.
application_angle: The work directly informs practical QEC stacks for imperfect superconducting hardware with device-specific noise and leakage.

title: Precision Limits of Multiparameter Markovian-Noise Metrology
abstract: Measuring stochastic signals ("noise metrology") constitutes a central task in quantum sensing and the characterization of open quantum systems. Here we establish ultimate precision bounds for multiparameter estimation of stochastic signals encoded through Markovian Lindblad dynamics, allowing for arbitrary quantum control and noiseless ancillae. Although Markovianity enforces standard-quantum-limit scaling with sensing time $T$, our bounds reveal Heisenberg-type scaling in the number of dissipative channels, $R$: when the stochastic signal exhibits high-rank correlations across the $R$ channels and the probe is entangled, the average variance (per parameter) scales no better than $Ω(1/(TR^2))$. For collective $k$-body dissipation, $R=Θ(N^k)$, signifying super-Heisenberg scaling with the system size $N$. We further show that, when the unknown parameters enter through the dissipative eigenrates, a Rapid Prepare-and-Measure (RPM) protocol that tracks many distinct quantum jumps in parallel attains these limits. In this regime, the estimation problem reduces to a multi-Poisson counting model, providing a conceptually clean route to optimal quantum noise metrology. We illustrate the breadth of the framework with applications to networked noise metrology, collective many-body dissipation, learning Pauli noise, and subdiffraction quantum imaging.
one_liner: Ultimate bounds for multiparameter Markovian noise metrology reveal channel-number Heisenberg scaling via entangled probes.
why_it_matters: It clarifies when quantum resources improve stochastic-signal sensing and gives an attainable protocol for optimal noise metrology.
detailed_summary: The paper derives precision limits for estimating multiple stochastic parameters encoded in Markovian Lindblad dynamics, allowing arbitrary control and noiseless ancillae. While Markovian evolution enforces standard scaling with sensing time, the bounds show Heisenberg-type scaling in the number of dissipative channels for high-rank correlated noise and entangled probes. For eigenrate estimation, a Rapid Prepare-and-Measure protocol reaches the limits by tracking many quantum jumps in parallel, reducing the task to multi-Poisson counting.
platform_or_system: controlled open quantum sensors with ancillae
main_result: The authors establish bounds scaling as Omega(1/(T R^2)) per parameter in favorable regimes and give an RPM protocol that attains them.
application_angle: The framework applies to networked sensing, many-body dissipation, Pauli-noise learning, and subdiffraction quantum imaging.

title: Low-valency scalable quantum error correction with a dynamic compass code
abstract: The ongoing development of hardware that is capable of reliably executing general quantum algorithms requires quantum error-correcting codes that are both practical for realisation and rapidly reduce logical error rates as they are scaled up. Here we introduce the dynamic compass code, a code that can be implemented with a modest footprint on the heavy-hex lattice while also demonstrating a threshold. The dynamic code is obtained by choosing a novel measurement schedule for the syndrome extraction circuit of the heavy-hex subsystem code. We numerically evaluate its performance and observe that different choices of schedule can provide a trade-off in protection against logical errors in the $X$ vs $Z$ basis. We also demonstrate that this new measurement schedule provides the code with a threshold for stability experiments. We finally show how the dynamic compass code could be used for fault-tolerant logic by illustrating lattice surgery between code patches.
one_liner: Dynamic compass scheduling gives heavy-hex quantum error correction a low-valency route to thresholds and lattice surgery.
why_it_matters: It targets hardware-native fault tolerance on heavy-hex devices with modest footprint, tunable X/Z protection, and explicit logic operations.
detailed_summary: The work introduces a dynamic compass code obtained by changing the syndrome-extraction schedule of the heavy-hex subsystem code. Numerical simulations show a threshold in stability experiments and reveal schedule-dependent tradeoffs between protection against logical X and Z errors. The authors also outline lattice surgery between patches, connecting the code design to fault-tolerant logical operations.
main_result: A low-valency dynamic compass code on heavy-hex geometry is shown numerically to have a threshold and support lattice-surgery-style fault-tolerant logic.
application_angle: The design is relevant to scalable quantum processors because it maps naturally to sparse heavy-hex connectivity while preserving practical QEC performance.

title: Cell-Dependent Criticality for Quantum Metrology
abstract: Exploiting enhanced sensitivity of a system in the vicinity of a phase transition boundary, critical quantum metrology to date still suffers from gap-closure related bottleneck effects, namely, critical slowing down of the sensing dynamics and a drastic shrinking of the parameter sensing window. To alleviate the said bottleneck inherent to any homogeneous lattice used for sensing, here we propose to leverage the intrinsic hopping inhomogeneity arising from bosonic ladder-operator matrix elements in Fock-space lattices (FSLs). Specifically, using a two-mode Jaynes--Cummings-type model, we show that the sensing parameter can be imprinted onto a topological zero-energy mode of the FSL. The key system parameters thus become cell dependent, effectively tracing out a curve in a topological phase diagram. Cell-dependent criticality emerges when this curve crosses or approaches a topological phase boundary, without globally tuning the lattice close to criticality. An external control parameter reshapes this curve, continuously tuning the scaling of the quantum Fisher information from the standard to the Heisenberg scaling while maintaining broad sensing coverage and a reduced gap cost. Furthermore, a local photon-number measurement on a single cavity saturates the quantum Fisher information. These results identify FSLs as a scalable and practical route to criticality-based quantum metrology.
one_liner: Fock-space lattice inhomogeneity enables critical quantum metrology without globally tuning the system to a narrow critical point.
why_it_matters: It offers a route to Heisenberg-like metrology with broader sensing coverage, lower gap penalty, and a simple local photon-number readout.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes using intrinsic hopping inhomogeneity in Fock-space lattices to create cell-dependent criticality for sensing. In a two-mode Jaynes-Cummings-type model, the sensed parameter is imprinted onto a topological zero-energy mode whose effective parameters trace a curve through a topological phase diagram. An external control tunes quantum Fisher information scaling from standard to Heisenberg-like behavior while keeping a broad sensing window, and local photon-number measurement can saturate the QFI.
main_result: The authors show that cell-dependent topological criticality can enhance quantum Fisher information while reducing critical slowing and sensing-window collapse.
application_angle: This could make criticality-enhanced photonic sensing more practical by avoiding the extreme fine tuning usually required near phase transitions.

title: Boson sampling beyond the dilute regime: second moments and anti-concentration
abstract: Boson sampling is a leading candidate for demonstrating quantum advantage in photonic systems. Despite significant experimental and theoretical progress, a characterization of its output statistics remains incomplete. This is especially true beyond the dilute regime, where photon collisions and bunching become significant. The associated saturated regime, characterized by mode number growing linearly with photon number, or more generally sub-quadratically, is precisely the regime of greatest experimental interest. As a consequence, anti-concentration of the output distribution--a key ingredient in hardness arguments--remains poorly understood in boson sampling. In this work, we leverage representation-theoretic tools to address this gap, obtaining closed-form expressions for second moments of generic particle-number-preserving bosonic observables. We express these quantities in terms of Hilbert-Schmidt norms of projections onto irreducible components of the operator space and show that these projection norms admit compact analytical expressions by exploiting the underlying symmetry structure. Focusing on Fock state output probabilities, we further establish anti-concentration beyond the dilute regime. Together with recent complexity-theoretic results, our findings strengthen hardness guarantees for boson sampling in experimentally interesting settings.
one_liner: Representation-theoretic second-moment formulas extend boson-sampling anti-concentration beyond the dilute regime.
why_it_matters: It strengthens theoretical support for photonic quantum advantage in the collision-rich regimes most relevant to experiments.
detailed_summary: The paper studies boson-sampling output statistics when the mode number is only linear or sub-quadratic in photon number, where collisions and bunching matter. It derives closed-form second moments for generic particle-number-preserving bosonic observables using irreducible decompositions of operator space. Applied to Fock output probabilities, the results establish anti-concentration beyond the dilute regime and complement recent hardness arguments.
main_result: Closed-form second moments are derived and used to prove anti-concentration for boson sampling in experimentally relevant saturated regimes.
application_angle: The result supports the credibility of near-term photonic quantum advantage demonstrations operating outside ideal dilute limits.

title: Design and Verification of a Terahertz Bandpass Filter using a Spoof Surface Plasmon Polariton Waveguide with Gapped Unit Cells
abstract: This paper presents the experimental verification of a planar guided-wave terahertz (THz) spoof surface plasmon polariton (SSPP) bandpass filter (BPF) using a coplanar stripline (CPS) with internal grooves and periodic gaps. The proposed BPF operates by combining the low-pass behavior from the SSPP's band edge and the high-pass behavior from the gaps that act as series capacitors. The higher and lower cut-off frequencies can be tailored by the appropriate selection of the unit cell geometry. For demonstration, a BPF with a center frequency of approximately 1 THz and a bandwidth of 0.25 THz was designed, fabricated, and experimentally validated. The passband around 1 THz is observed in the measurements, along with the lower and higher cut-off frequencies at approximately 0.91 THz and 1.16 THz, respectively, in agreement with simulation results.
one_liner: A planar spoof-plasmon THz bandpass filter is fabricated and measured with a passband centered near 1 THz.
why_it_matters: It provides an experimentally validated, geometry-tunable THz filtering element for integrated guided-wave photonic systems.
detailed_summary: The paper designs and verifies a terahertz bandpass filter based on a coplanar stripline spoof surface plasmon polariton waveguide with internal grooves and periodic gaps. The SSPP band edge supplies low-pass behavior, while the gaps act as series capacitors that set high-pass behavior. A fabricated device shows a passband around 1 THz with measured cutoffs near 0.91 THz and 1.16 THz, matching simulations.
main_result: A fabricated SSPP bandpass filter demonstrates a roughly 1 THz center frequency and 0.25 THz bandwidth with measured cutoffs consistent with design.
application_angle: Compact, tunable THz filters are useful for spectroscopy, imaging, communications, and integrated terahertz photonic circuits.

title: Dual-mode ground-state cooling in quadratic optomechanical systems: from multistability to general dark-mode suppression
abstract: We theoretically investigate a quadratic optomechanical system comprising a single-mode optical cavity linearly coupled to one mechanical resonator and quadratically coupled to a second resonator. By tuning the cavity detuning and optomechanical coupling strengths, we demonstrate the transition from optical bistability to multistability with up to seven steady-state solutions. Notably, simultaneous ground-state cooling of both mechanical resonators occurs on the dynamically stable branch of the nonlinear steady-state solutions, offering new opportunities for combined nonlinear optical and quantum cooling functionalities. Beyond the multistable regime, we systematically study dual-mode ground-state cooling and find that robust simultaneous cooling can be achieved over a broad parameter range, except when the linear and quadratic couplings become comparable, where a dark-mode effect arises. In this case, tuning the second-order optomechanical-induced frequency shifts effectively suppresses dark-mode interference, enabling controllable and simultaneous ground-state cooling. Our results provide a versatile framework for engineering multimode quantum states in optomechanical systems and open new avenues for the development of multifunctional quantum devices, including ultra-sensitive sensors, scalable quantum memories, and integrated quantum networks.
one_liner: Quadratic optomechanics enables controllable dual-resonator ground-state cooling with dark-mode suppression.
why_it_matters: Simultaneous cooling of multiple mechanical modes is a key primitive for optomechanical sensors, memories, and quantum network interfaces.
detailed_summary: The work analyzes a single-mode optical cavity coupled linearly to one mechanical resonator and quadratically to another. By tuning detuning and coupling strengths, the system moves from bistability to multistability with up to seven steady-state solutions, including dynamically stable branches supporting simultaneous ground-state cooling. The study identifies a dark-mode limitation when couplings are comparable and shows that second-order optomechanical frequency shifts can suppress the interference.
main_result: The authors theoretically demonstrate robust simultaneous ground-state cooling of two mechanical modes and a control method for suppressing dark-mode interference.
application_angle: The scheme could improve multimode quantum-state preparation for precision sensing, quantum memories, and integrated quantum communication nodes.

title: Hydration Monitoring Using Urinary Biomarkers: A Hybrid Classical Quantum Predictive Modeling Framework
abstract: Hydration status is a key physiological indicator associated with cellular homeostasis, renal function, and overall health. Recent advances in smart sensing environments enable passive monitoring of urinary biomarkers that can provide continuous insight into hydration dynamics. In this work, we investigate predictive modeling approaches for hydration monitoring using biomarker data collected through the Predict Health Toilet (PHT) system. The problem is formulated as a regression task using urinary indicators such as urine specific gravity, conductivity, and volume. We evaluate classical machine learning models and quantum machine learning architectures based on variational quantum circuits. In particular, we introduce a modular Quantum Sequential Model (QSM) designed to construct flexible hybrid quantum classical predictive pipelines. Experimental results compare classical regression models, symmetry-constrained quantum regressors, and QSM architectures. The results provide insights into the potential role of quantum machine learning in digital health monitoring systems and highlight the opportunities and current limitations of near-term quantum computing for physiological data analysis.
one_liner: Hybrid quantum-classical regressors are tested for hydration prediction from urinary biomarker data.
why_it_matters: It probes a concrete digital-health sensing use case for near-term quantum machine learning, including its practical limits.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates hydration monitoring as regression over urinary biomarkers such as specific gravity, conductivity, and volume collected by the Predict Health Toilet system. It compares classical machine learning models with variational quantum regressors and a modular Quantum Sequential Model for hybrid quantum-classical pipelines. The results assess where near-term quantum models may help and where current architectures remain limited for physiological data analysis.
platform_or_system: hybrid VQC models on urinary biomarker data
main_result: A modular quantum sequential modeling framework is evaluated against classical regressors for predicting hydration status from smart-toilet biomarker measurements.
application_angle: The study connects quantum machine learning to continuous health sensing, while clarifying the maturity gap for deployment on near-term quantum hardware.

title: Linear Optical Schemes to Postselect High-Dimensional Dicke States
abstract: Multipartite entanglement is an essential quantum resource for various distributed quantum applications. One promising method for preparing multipartite entanglement is to interfere independent photons at linear optical interference setups. While heralding the successful interference and thereby the state generation is often costly, postselecting entangled states provides an achievable alternative in this framework. We introduce a family of interference schemes for postselecting symmetric qudit Dicke states, useful resources in quantum communication and variational quantum computing. We present schemes with and without ancillary photons and show that using ancillary photons can exceed the upper bound on the success probability of schemes without ancillary photons. Our results accommodate a wide range of linear optical schemes, providing multiple viable approaches for postselecting Dicke states.
one_liner: Linear-optical circuits are proposed to postselect high-dimensional symmetric qudit Dicke states from interfering photons.
why_it_matters: The schemes target experimentally accessible multipartite qudit entanglement for quantum networks and variational photonic processing.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a family of linear-optical interference schemes for postselecting symmetric high-dimensional Dicke states. It compares constructions with and without ancillary photons, showing that ancilla-assisted schemes can surpass success-probability bounds that apply to non-ancilla setups. The work frames these states as resources for distributed quantum communication and variational quantum computing.
main_result: The authors derive multiple postselection schemes for symmetric qudit Dicke states, including ancilla-assisted designs with improved success probability.
application_angle: More efficient postselected entanglement generation can support near-term photonic network protocols without full heralding overhead.

title: Observation of Tunable Superradiant Frequency Combs
abstract: Cavity quantum electrodynamics (QED) with quantum emitters coupled to resonators provides a powerful platform for engineering light-matter interactions and exploring collective phenomena. In particular, superradiance, arising from collective quantum interference among emitters, has been explored as a route to ultrastable continuous radiation. However, engineering superradiance in the time domain to realize periodic pulsed sources or frequency combs remains largely unexplored. Here, we investigate the non-equilibrium many-body dynamics of a driven spin ensemble coupled to an on-chip superconducting resonator and uncover a dynamical phase transition from continuous-wave to periodic pulsed superradiant emission. To quantitatively capture the observed dynamical phases, we introduce a driven-dissipative cavity-QED model that elucidates how the periodic pulsed superradiant phase emerges from collective, periodically repeating spin dynamics stabilized by the interplay of coherence growth, disorder, and dissipation. We also find that rare-earth ion spin systems exhibiting both optical and microwave transitions enable phase-synchronized, dual-rail superradiant frequency combs in both the microwave and optical domains. Our results not only open new avenues for dual-rail frequency-comb applications in quantum metrology and information processing, but also establish a fundamental connection between periodic pulsed superradiance and the emergence of a continuous time crystal as a novel nonequilibrium phase in driven open systems.
one_liner: A driven spin ensemble in a superconducting resonator shows a tunable transition from continuous to pulsed superradiant comb emission.
why_it_matters: Superradiant frequency combs could provide coherent, low-noise microwave and optical references for quantum metrology and information systems.
detailed_summary: The work reports a dynamical phase transition in a driven spin ensemble coupled to an on-chip superconducting resonator, moving from continuous-wave superradiance to periodic pulsed emission. A driven-dissipative cavity-QED model explains the phase through repeated collective spin dynamics stabilized by coherence growth, disorder, and dissipation. The authors further identify rare-earth ion systems as candidates for phase-synchronized microwave and optical superradiant combs.
platform_or_system: spin ensemble coupled to superconducting resonator
main_result: The study observes tunable periodic pulsed superradiance forming a frequency comb in a driven cavity-QED platform.
application_angle: A synchronized dual-domain comb source would be useful for precision timing, frequency metrology, and hybrid quantum interconnects.

title: Quantum Landscape of Superconducting Diodes
abstract: This study maps the quantum landscape of superconducting diodes (SDs) \cite{nadeem23} onto the quantum technology architecture, which is currently constrained by fundamental challenges in control and scalability. In the existing non-integrated quantum technology hardware, control and scalability related issues emerge at two fronts: First, nonlinear and nonreciprocal circuit elements, which are essential building blocks for quantum processors, are often complex, bulky, and dissipative. Second, the temperature gradient between classical control electronics ($T_C\gtrsim$ K), which is also dissipative, and the quantum processor at cryogenic temperatures ($T_Q\sim$ mK) makes scalability even more challenging. The main focus is to reveal how the built-in nonlinearity, nonreciprocity, and quantum functionalities of SDs are significant for on-chip integrated circuit quantum electrodynamics (c-QED), enabling scalable integration of noise-resilient qubit and qubit-interfaces for efficient power delivery, coherent control and memory, high-fidelity readout, and quantum-limited amplification. To this end, this study will also shed light on how thermodynamic constraints and field effects can be harnessed within a quantum-enhanced SD platform, thereby enabling thermal compatibility between classical and quantum workflows, isothermal all-electrical control, and on-chip scalability. This perspective is expected to play a pivotal role in the advancement of superconducting circuit-based quantum hardware with temperature-matched classical, quantum, and hybrid workflows.
one_liner: A perspective maps superconducting diodes onto scalable circuit-QED hardware functions for control, readout, and amplification.
why_it_matters: Built-in nonreciprocity and nonlinearity could reduce bulky cryogenic components and improve integration of superconducting quantum processors.
detailed_summary: The paper positions superconducting diodes as integrated nonlinear and nonreciprocal elements for superconducting quantum technology. It discusses their potential roles in qubit interfaces, power delivery, coherent control, memory, high-fidelity readout, and quantum-limited amplification. The perspective also emphasizes thermal and field-effect engineering for more compatible classical-quantum workflows at cryogenic temperatures.
main_result: The study outlines how superconducting diodes could act as compact on-chip components for scalable circuit-QED architectures.
application_angle: Replacing complex dissipative control hardware with integrated superconducting elements could ease cryogenic wiring, heat load, and scaling constraints.
